edges.description: High-grade lesions emerge through disruption of p53/Rb-regulated programs
Progressive tumors acquire invasion, repopulation, and treatment-resistance programs
Additional alterations convert high-grade intraepithelial disease into invasive carcinoma
Plasticity and genomic context influence chemotherapy response and later-line treatment selection

edges.target: Adaptive Immune Resistance and PD-L1-Mediated Immune Evasion

nodes.description: Bladder urothelial carcinoma is an immunogenic tumor in which tumor cells and the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment co-opt the PD-1/PD-L1 homeostatic axis. Interferon-gamma from infiltrating effector T cells drives adaptive PD-L1 upregulation, and PD-1 engagement on CD8+ T cells suppresses cytotoxic anti-tumor activity, enabling immune escape. This actively suppressed but pre-existing anti-tumor immunity is the mechanistic rationale for PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint blockade across non-muscle-invasive and advanced urothelial carcinoma.
nodes.id: Adaptive Immune Resistance and PD-L1-Mediated Immune Evasion

biological_processes: Negative Regulation of T Cell Mediated Immunity

evidence.snippet: The interaction of PD-1 and PD-L1 negatively regulates adaptive immune response mainly by inhibiting the activity of effector T cells while enhancing the function of immunosuppressive regulatory T cells (Tregs), largely contributing to the maintenance of immune homeostasis that prevents dysregulated immunity and harmful immune responses. However, cancer cells exploit the PD-1/PD-L1 axis to cause immune escape in cancer development and progression.

evidence.reference_title: Recent advances in immunotherapy for bladder cancer: mechanisms, clinical applications, and future perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Key determinants of the tumor microenvironment (TME)-such as immunosuppressive cell populations, regulatory cytokines, and metabolic barriers-are examined in the context of their roles in mediating therapeutic resistance.

nodes.description: Progression to muscle-invasive and metastatic disease is accompanied by epithelial plasticity, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition programs, and urothelial cancer stem-like behavior. These programs contribute to heterogeneous tumor formation, resistance to systemic therapy, and early repopulation after treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Epithelial plasticity in urothelial carcinoma: Current advancements and future challenges.
evidence.snippet: Urothelial cancer stem cells (UroCSCs), a tumor subpopulation derived from transformation of urothelial stem cells, are responsible for heterogeneous tumor formation and resistance to systemic treatment in UC of the bladder.

evidence.reference_title: Epithelial plasticity in urothelial carcinoma: Current advancements and future challenges.
evidence.snippet: transcriptome analysis of microdissected cancer cells expressing multiple progenitor/stem cell markers validates the upregulation of genes that derive epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition.

nodes.description: PD-1/PD-L1-directed immunotherapy is used across advanced urothelial carcinoma and increasingly across earlier disease states depending on clinical context.

evidence.reference_title: Recent advances in immunotherapy for bladder cancer: mechanisms, clinical applications, and future perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) targeting PD-1/PD-L1 and CTLA-4, adoptive cellular therapies including chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy, oncolytic viruses, and novel immunomodulatory agents have transformed the therapeutic landscape for both non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) and advanced urothelial carcinoma (UC).

nodes.description: Advanced urothelial carcinoma contains gene-expression-defined molecular subtypes and actionable genomic alterations that increasingly guide therapy. FGFR3 alterations, DNA damage repair alterations, and broader transcriptional classes can shape prognosis and predict response to chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and targeted treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Evolving Role of Genomics in Genitourinary Neoplasms.
evidence.snippet: Urothelial carcinoma can be classified into different subtypes based on gene expression profiling, which provides prognostic information and predicts response to chemotherapy and immunotherapy.

evidence.reference_title: Evolving Role of Genomics in Genitourinary Neoplasms.
evidence.snippet: Specific mutations have been identified that predict response to therapy including ERCC2 mutations and cisplatin, DNA damage and repair mutations and checkpoint inhibitors, and FGFR3 mutations and FGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as erdafitinib.

nodes.description: High-grade carcinoma in situ and aggressive invasive bladder urothelial carcinoma are closely linked to inactivation of p53- and Rb-governed tumor suppressor programs. Experimental modeling shows that p53 loss alone is not sufficient for invasive progression, indicating that additional cooperating events are required.

biological_processes: negative regulation of G1/S transition of mitotic cell cycle

evidence.reference_title: Loss of p53 and acquisition of angiogenic microRNA profile are insufficient to facilitate progression of bladder urothelial carcinoma in situ to invasive carcinoma.
evidence.snippet: Inactivation of tumor suppressor p53 and pRb in urothelium by SV40 T antigen resulted in urothelial carcinoma, resembling human high-grade carcinoma in situ.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of p53 and acquisition of angiogenic microRNA profile are insufficient to facilitate progression of bladder urothelial carcinoma in situ to invasive carcinoma.
evidence.snippet: Collectively, our results support the notion that activation of angiogenesis and loss of p53 are not sufficient for progression to invasive cancer.

nodes.description: The overwhelming majority of bladder carcinomas arise from the urothelium and present along a clinical spectrum from non-muscle-invasive to muscle-invasive and metastatic disease. Depth of invasion is a central determinant of prognosis and treatment intensity.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of urothelial carcinoma of the bladder.
evidence.snippet: More than 90% of bladder carcinomas are classified as urothelial carcinoma (UC), which arise from the urothelium.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of urothelial carcinoma of the bladder.
evidence.snippet: Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder may present as a non-muscle-invasive, muscle-invasive, or metastatic malignancy.
